sentences.text: Oh, like I tell you, it was 1942, before the holidays.
That was 28 or 30 or the 1st of September when the-- before the killing.
I went away from the ghetto.
I just reminded before.
And I says my mother, goodbye.
And my wife was that time with a little baby.
I went-- bye.
And my mother says, I hope you're going see-- you're going to see the terrible tragedy world which is today on.
And I hope it's going to be a different world which I don't see after.
And we share each other and came to the-- ghetto-- out.
They did-- the guy let-- don't let us out.
How he-- but he bring in the Jews.
And suddenly, they let me out.
I just have to finish the work.
As I tell you before, we went out.
And that's the last time I see her with my wife.
And that's it.
My brother went with me together.
It was five people.
My brother went with me.
We worked together.
As I told you last time, my brother-- I, I was-- I had hope he's going to live-- he's living.
But when I see this guy which I could call him up from here, another guy which was together with me also from the-- run away from the-- is-- I thought-- we were 10 people, three women and seven men.
We run away.
And we chart off in the bandits-- in the forest.
And the front-- the free-- he was running in front of me, he took a package.
I said-- I said, don't take any package.
And I had my package.
I didn't take it.
I run after him like this.
He told me, run away.
When you're running away, try to get this.
I run away.
Other of them says, [?
Simcha, ?] why don't you run?
He says, I can't run.
I'm, I'm finished.
I run away, hide myself.
This guy which is over here with me-- I could call him up-- he run away.
I didn't know about them all together.
And I planning to go back with the snow in January 9, 1944.
I'm planning to go back.
And suddenly, I see the end from the forest.
And then from the forest, the other side, the, the Bandera couldn't come.
Over there is Polish.
I have to go on the other forest.
I couldn't come.
And when I see the end to the forest, I went to the guy-- this side of the forest, I went to our guy.
He says to me-- he says, better go away because the Bandera, they are coming to me.
Where you came from?
I says, come from, from, from the ghetto.
I lived in the ghetto.
He says, if you want to stay, stay over here.
That's no good.
He gave me at least a bed, huh?
I went on the other side.
I didn't know where I go.
I went-- straw.
It was already 11 o'clock at night.
I went in the straw.
And I laid down in the straw.
And suddenly comes Germans.
And they're speaking German.
If they were Ukrainish, I don't know who's this.
They're taking hay, straw to the-- for the cows.
And they're going, going, going deeper and deeper and deeper, close to me already.
They went away.
When they went away, I run away to the-- to the forest deeper.
I didn't know-- I don't know where I go.
I went away.
And suddenly, I see another little house there.
I go to the little house.
It's a mother with two kids running away-- running away from me.
He says-- stays with me.
I was nice dressed-- beautiful [INAUDIBLE].
Then says in Polish, come away, come back.
I says, that's not where they live.
I want to run away for the kids.
They're staying.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], this is a Jew.
I'll pass.
We could make-- you better investigate.
I says, come on over here, in Polish [SPEAKING POLISH], I'm a Jew.
And she come back.
She give me to eat, to drink.
And she says, in the daytime, you work over here with the chicken, by the herds, by that.
At night, we always sleeping over there.
But, if you want to go, is over there-- go three blocks-- is there Jewish people.
We call it Ukrainish on the other side.
And over here is Polacks.
And over there is Israel.
[LAUGHS] I came back there.
I see a family, Shreiber, which is supposed to be over here in America-- Shreiber-- and three, four people from my town which are-- some of them is in Israel.
Some I know in the [INAUDIBLE]-- 24.
And they have a gun.
And they have two hand grenades, you know, not big with that.
I says to my friend of mine, which was in Israel-- anyway, I like-- she's got a nice place.
But I don't like to stay at night over here [INAUDIBLE].
Then we went to make a place to hide ourself at night.
That's-- in the daytime, we had corn, wheat-- we're--
--grinding.
OK.
My brother-- we've been together till January 9, 1944, together with this guy over here--
--running away from the-- from the bandit-- from the Bandera with leather.
And we're sitting together and talking about everything else-- 12 o'clock.
We're talking about-- it was two guys.
He's, he's a-- he's an [?
Argentine, ?] a Jew.
His name is Pesach-- Paul, Paul Diamond.
Paul Diamond is-- was living in years-- 20 years ago in Baltimore.
And in Baltimore, he was living.
He was looking for me.
He come back to me twice.
He was looking for me.
He came.
I was working.
And then I, I lost his address.
And I remember his address-- Paul Diamond in Baltimore.
He was a, a goy-- not-- I was-- but a-- but a-- [?
Ukrainishes ?] goy.
He used to come every Sunday to us.
And we had lunches.
And Sunday, we have a beautiful lunch, potatoes and pear-- everything.
OK.
Paul Diamond in Baltimore.
That's the main thing.
My brother-- we run-- nine, there are nine.
We run away, nine of us-- he said, 10 of us.
And in forest, the bandits, they run away after us.
And they caught us, all of us, sitting together.
But the story when they caught us, was this guy was a Jew.
And the other guy didn't know he was Jewish.
Then he says to me-- used to say, the guy, which is a religious guy, and didn't eat meat.
He had-- make [INAUDIBLE].
He says to me, [NON-ENGLISH] you see him.
And you know how many Jewish he killed?
He says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], [INAUDIBLE] when he sees.
And we didn't-- I, I-- we didn't tell him he was Jewish.
He didn't-- we-- he didn't know.
Three, four people didn't know.
Because we didn't like the way they talked.
But we had the secret of it.
I-- finally-- then, owner is an Argentine, which she went to him.
And he hide her.
And she was-- and he's living in [?
Argentine ?] with her brother.
Do you understand?
When was the last time you saw your brother?
Then we went together-- all together in forest, all together.
We're sitting together, 12 o'clock at night, January 9, 1944.
The bandits are around us.
And we run away from them.
And this guy, which is over here, run away this side.
And the other guy says, oh, [NON-ENGLISH] gave this-and-this guy.
And I run after him.
Before him, was-- snowed.
I run after him, and he got shot.
Because he was carrying-- I told him not to carry anything.
He-- no.
Are don't know with my brother.
I didn't see him.
I didn't see him anymore after--
--that.
I remember after that, which I told you, I had--
After the ghetto when?
I was hiding, no?
Hiding for awhile, yeah.
Yeah, the Ukrainians caught me.
They need me for the work.
And they didn't yell.
They kill the-- the bosses they killed because I was waiting for the Polish, I-- but was Ukrainish.
Yeah, yeah, Bandera was later on developed.
We didn't know Bandera.
But they are developed before.
And later on, they know I'm working.
And they know they're going to catch me.
And then, when they killed the Polacks, they took the leather.
Let me ask you something.
They have-- the Polacks have leather [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is leather, you know that.
The [NON-ENGLISH] is leather for crafts.
They have hided about 10, 12, inside the border-- all materials are hided and buried down, hided between them.
When I took over the whole thing, [NON-ENGLISH], they call it, the whole area which belongs to me, then we find that [NON-ENGLISH], which we hided.
We bury it down.
And he was-- I was the manager from the leather business.
I had a place.
You have to give a leather, whatever.
If he comes with a-- and this, this guy, I have to give out two pair leather, pair shoes, three pair soles, three pair this.
Then once I had by the Bandera, [?you know?] ?
We made a good living down there.
But we know that they give us eat at best-- Did they know you were Jewish?
Oh, sure.
[LAUGHS] They know me from childhood.
It didn't matter.
They need me.
They need this doctor, which I can call him up.
If you want to, call him-- the telephone.
They need him.
They, they kept him.
They kept him-- two months after he got the Russian occupied, they kept them, still kept him.
They watch him.
The town Lokacze, in the forest.
Working in the factory for what?
For them--
The Ukrainish factory was-- the factory was in two places that way.
It was-- Ukrainish factory was-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] oy, yi, yi, yi, yi.
I'll, I'll remind myself.
I have to check in the book.
The factory was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] close to Kupiczow.
Kupiczow was 3,000 Polacks with Jews together hided-- Kupiczow.
And Ozeryani, Ozeryani, was a city with the girls from them.
And [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], was the factory.
[NON-ENGLISH] was the factory.
But-- and, and they have problems.
They have 3,000-- the Polacks, they have 3,000 people in Ozeryani there.
And they have a tank.
And they took a tank-- one Sunday, they took a tank with 30 or 40 or 50-some soldiers in the Wolyn, in the old village.
Soldier-- how do you say the village?
The village is named-- Kupiczow?
Ozyerani.
No, not Ozeryani.
Kupiczow?
They say the city Kupiczow, Kupiczow.
But then, in the Poland, in the-- in-- got killed 90 Ukrainish.
It was Sunday, July-- June, July, I think June or July-- 90, and that 14th or 15th June.
But they took over the whole thing.
And some Jews was down there, probably to hide them.
Are you going to write this in the paper?
Some Jews is alive.
They could know this.
The name of the-- Kupiczow was the little town, three town in Poland.
They have a problem.
And they took a tank with people, with all army, and they took away Kupiczow because there was right close to them.
There were-- and they bring in the-- Sunday, they bring those dead people.
And I was there by the funeral.
Come to mother.
She's telling you. 90 people, 90 dead, one on the other, which-- she was also-- the lady which I'm trying to tell you-- she also was there, all the-- all the people.
And we see that.
And they took her-- the whole city, Kupiczow.
They took the city with-- they all-- and some from the-- after the war, I spoke to a guy.
He says, I was down there.
We run away.
Well, they have ammunition, a lot of ammunition. 3,000 people, they have plenty of ammunition, the Polacks.
Yeah.
I was working in factory--
--since that time I'm telling you--
--work in the factory.
For he-- they was playing theater.
One time, they were playing theater.
And they didn't send us tickets in the play there.
I said, everybody has tickets in theater, and I didn't-- me.
Then this guy over here, he says, I'm going to see.
I cannot go.
I go, sit with them.
I didn't have a seat.
Stand aside because seats was like-- you're making seats in the theater, one-- about 30 seats, 40 seats.
And we stand in the side like other people standing in the side.
They didn't care about us.
And I didn't care about them.
They was playing, like, a Jew.
That [NON-ENGLISH] comes in.
He has two workers.
And he has a wife, a family.
And he has whiskey for sale.
He had this for sale.
And a girl comes in.
I eat a piece of bread?
And he take a stake, and he hit the girl.
And she falls apart, and things like this.
Other things they're playing.
Everything to show them up.
They shouldn't [INAUDIBLE].
But the end from the story comes up.
We live in that world which you see over here.
We try to avoid it, that kind of world.
But anybody has a Jew, anybody has a Polack, anybody has something, it has to be reported.
We have to watch it.
Because is-- change is coming, and we know who is this.
This end of the story said.
And we went home.
We went home.
After a couple weeks later, there was a-- November, November or December, they killed everybody else.
Only us is alive.
And those two girls we [INAUDIBLE] there, they, they went away.
They went-- run away.
And we-- I thought we-- they, they are dead.
But that guy-- the man that had-- [NON-ENGLISH] his name, the-- really name is [NON-ENGLISH].
He says, they're living, you know.
Don't say anything.
They are living.
They are hided.
And after the war, I met those girls over here in Lodz.
Listen, how could you keep surviving?
I lost a brother, the worst part-- which I feel terrible.
But the living which I've been living, as my friend said, Elze Gellar, he told me the same stories which I passed through, putting the salt on the eyes.
Did I tell you the salt, about salt?
Did I tell you?
When the police, they'd have to bring us in-- into the city, then we had salt in the eyes.
And I used to put the one salt.
And the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] I said in Hebrew.
And I put the salt, [NON-ENGLISH], but for the second.
And I run away by putting in the eyes the salt.
And no-- the, the one shot me over here on the side.
That was in 1942.
It was before the holidays.
Didn't I tell you before the same story?
Not this part of it.
Yeah.
And then we went away.
And then we-- my brother run with the other one--
--the guy on-- down there.
And I was one-- and one caught-- they caught alive.
And we meet each other at 1 o'clock at night.
And we start to-- again, to work for life.
It was very hard.
Oh, the partisans--
I don't know.
But that's up to you.
I know the, the-- you, you--
Before I go to the partisans, the-- all I'm interested-- that the, the way I got with the gypsies, the way I run away, the way they-- that's the policeman, [NON-ENGLISH].
Gretzner come.
And he says, hide yourself.
And I run.
He said, the boy, just leave it there.
They kill the-- I see.
Another-- [INAUDIBLE] after the dead.
They-- this guy took again a picture from them.
But he has to pull with the Gestapo.
And this is laying down there.
And he says to them, don't shoot anymore.
And that's it.
What else could I tell you?
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every day, the daven, this time of the year, we are living the worst time-- Passover, for these holidays, one of the worst time.
Then we run away in the forest, we start to hunger.
May-- you know what hunger is.
We start to walk in the forest.
We'd eat for three days nothing.
We couldn't eat.
And then we lay down.
You couldn't get up, couldn't walk.
It's two miles in the forest, we couldn't walk.
Then suddenly, one guy, which is, is a actor I told you about, Shell Perlmutter, he had a wife over here in this country.
He says, you know what is there?
I see there.
I walk a little bit.
And, and I see carrots.
What?
How could he see?
Yeah, carrots and beets.
We-- he, he couldn't walk.
And we got down on our knees, you know.
We had carrots and peas and everything else.
And we start to eat, well, about 12 o'clock at night.
We had carrots, beets-- were some bitter, you know.
We eat this up an hour.
And we took-- everybody took in their pockets.
And we start to walk like we didn't feel anything.
That juice from that beets, that juice from that vegetable give so much power, yeah, everybody start to get up.
We start to walk.
Oop, sorry, sorry.
We start to walk.
We walk to the forest.
We came to the forest.
It was a day after-- it was from three, four towns.
It was about a thousand-- over a thousand people resided before the war was-- halls.
In that halls was already over a thousand people.
And the-- and they make-- the, the Germans with the Ukrainish, they kill all of them.
But some-- three people, people run away.
They told us the story.
We didn't know.
We know everything is dead there.
But three, four people run away.
We come in there.
We says, OK, we could stay another week or two.
But it's danger.
At night, we have to go away.
It was November, 1942.
November 1942, we go here, we go there.
We get a family, which the children is in Israel, [NON-ENGLISH] [?
Adem ?] with [NON-ENGLISH] [?
Adem. ?] We find them there, in the forest.
He says, come on to me.
Sit there while you're in the hall.
We have this.
We have this.
We have-- took bullets.
And then, sit over here.
They give me a potato.
They give me-- I'm not so hungry because we had this one over here.
We went together two days.
I says, I cannot do it.
We have to go look for some difference-- people.
There were six, seven people in all.
Then he says to me, you know, I have a friend which I, I give you.
But like he says, he's looking for a tanner.
They find me.
I says, give me the name of him.
Let me show.
I'll show you.
Stay where you are.
I slept over a night-- the morning.
And-- I'll show you.
And this guy looking for me, he has leather.
He started, and, you know, he's looking for me.
And he heard about me.
He didn't tell me.
And suddenly, he find another place.
And he went to another place.
And the other place, right, they kept him two days.
And right away, they reported it to the police.
And the Nazis came, and they killed him-- [?
Adem. ?] They killed him.
I, I, I walked by myself.
And I had that hole over here in the back.
It was terrible pain.
Me-- and my brother had the, the bullets over here, pain.
And the pain is only-- with warm, steady temperature, that heals, heals.
But at night, you go out in the warm-- and during-- gets-- be cold.
It's terrible.
It's pain.
It doesn't heal.
And over here, a little thing didn't heal.
I went in a house-- Chelbourg.
And I asked the guy, give me a little rag.
Could you get a rag, everything?
They give me a rag.
They clean me up.
But they put me a little-- a little alcohol.
And the alcohol is brilliant for that one.
And I feel better.
And the same thing with him.
And suddenly, we want to go out-- stays two guys.
The name I remember if you need a name-- [NON-ENGLISH] and Chelbourg.
Because they told me name later.
He says, halt, stay!
You've got bullets.
You've got this.
And they checked me.
I had that, that dowry which my-- $500 which my, my-- they gave me dowry, you know that.
Then I had $200 for my brother over here and $300 over here.
It was only $50 bill, $20 bills.
American money was long-- very long.
You don't know that.
It was before you were born-- longer, a lot longer.
They call it macaroni [INAUDIBLE].
And they started to, to count the, the-- they are five people already.
And the guy hit me over here-- over here, with the stick-- with the potatoes, over here hit me.
And the blood has did this-- [NON-ENGLISH] [?
Chetner. ?] And my brother, they take-- one takes him in the corner.
And he says to him, over here.
He took out $200.
He had boots.
He put the $200.
He says, just try to live.
You've got everything.
I had leather.
I had needles.
I had cotton.
And since today, I put-- always I have cotton with me with a needle.
Till today on, I had always, you see, [LAUGHS] with me a cotton with needle over here, you see.
Because it reminds me that stuff-- anything, a button falls off, I've got right away with me a cotton and a needle.
And they took away all the things.
And they changed the money-- and they count the money, $30, $40, $10 bills-- what?
Never-- not dollar bills, so $10, $15.
And the-- and this guy has the $200.
What do we do with the Jews?
Kill them?
He get me over here.
He, he, he knows-- and the guy shares-- and the lady says-- yell, don't kill them in my apartment!
I don't want to see-- I don't want to see this, any of this!
He says to the husband-- these are strong guys-- he says, stop it, [?
Bela! ?] And they listened to her.
He says, no.
They thought a plan to bring me, like, to the soldiers that they had from the city.
They tie us up.
And they put us in a wagon.
And they bring me to the city-- to the place which I had on the city.
It was 12 o'clock at night.
One guy-- this guy goes to this.
And this guy, which he took the money, he tied me up over here.
He says, run.
He tied me up, my-- I was naked.
Well, December 6, 1942, cold-- the finger-- but we had one shoe, one sole-- one shoe.
[BACKROUND NOISE] And right away, the guy told me-- which I told Elze Gellar-- you don't have a pair of shoes.
One-- this is because this is dead.
Then I was wearing a boots.
And he was wearing a shoe.
They see I have not, not a pair.
He have not pair.
The shoes, they let me off.
There-- I was lucky.
They let me off, the shoes.
And he says, run.
You run.
Where are we going to go naked?
I mean, naked-- a little shirt-- a little shirt, torn.
We run.
Who-- how much could you run this [INAUDIBLE] snow, half a mile?
We're going city-- going up to houses-- see houses there, going in in this-- in the backs-- in the back to the house-- not in the house, in the straw.
We lay down.
And the dog's barking and barking and barking.
The guy comes out-- Andre.
He comes out.
He recognized me, you know.
He said, oh, you, you, you was there last year over here, I remember.
I have a couple of children.
And he says-- he bring us a-- potatoes.
He just add a pig fat.
He bring us that fresh.
I ate potatoes with fat, with meat.
And we eat it up.
And he says, come on in the house for awhile, for another two hours.
In the house-- he has his own, own, little house.
And he has a bed.
And all the kids in the bed, and two-- one little babies, five years old, sleeping with them.
It's early in the morning, 5 o'clock in the morning-- into the bed.
And he's told the mom this story.
We caught Jews.
What kind of Jews?
They were forced.
They this, they that.
Mommy, is that-- they big like a-- like a mouse, like a kitty?
No, they are people.
And he's laying.
Suddenly, he comes up.
He wants to see who's there into the bed.
One guy sees up-- my end or my brother's end.
Mommy, I see that.
He has hands like a human, like a man.
Five fingers, he's got-- and five fingers over here.
She-- says the mom-- she says, yeah, he has five fingers.
He's, like, human, like we are.
But he killed Jesus Christ.
They killed Jesus Christ.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The mother told me.
She-- Jesus Christ, if they killed.
Sure, he has to be got-- yeah, yes, they're bad-- killed.
They have to get killed.
The government doesn't like them, doesn't want them because they killed Jesus Christ.
Finally, 6, 7 o'clock, we went out back to the place.
The kids didn't see it.
And they let us up in the roof.
In the roof, up into the-- and, oh, we had cherries-- dried cherries and peaches and, and, you know, things like this.
We eat that.
We are not hunger-- you know, like this, they should give us a lunch.
And we couldn't get in bed.
This day we went up in the roof.
And later, we went out back to the horses, to the cows.
There was two cows.
It was warmer there.
And I was naked.
And they give me a thing to, to put up.
And I took-- had a needle with cotton a little bit.
Then I took-- made by myself a, a sleeve, and make by myself a hat, and tie down the hat, and take myself buttons-- from wood, I make buttons.
And I make a coat by myself.
And into the coat I had-- like, well, you know, any kind of junk, little rags, should be lower.
And I had all covered up.
And the foot, over here, I had the shoe.
And he had the boots.
We are complete.
Then he kept us two weeks.
He says, the, the Germans looking for who killed pigs.
You have to go away.
Where are you going to go?
He went-- he went away.
And we followed to that gate with these guys, they were-- run away.
Yeah, he says, go.
And I went to this guy, which I told you, the next door, just three blocks.
I came and knocking the door.
And the letter said, [?
Hamka. ?] He says to me-- he says, I'm looking for this guy-- this and this guy, that [?
Garber, ?] for months and months.
And these people I had into my house.
They was here, and I give them bread-- [?
Adems, ?] I told you.
And they told me they have a guy-- they have me.
They know me.
They're going to bring me.
He says-- and the Einsatz, they're very smart.
He says, look at this, what Jews are-- he's so tricky.
When I says, I need you.
And he says, he isn't.
And I told him, yeah.
He says, how would I prove it?
He says, these guys-- and she wants to keep it to make the leather.
She want to keep us because the other one, she let him go.
And right away, they kill them, the third day, the other one.
And she says to me-- I says to her this way, I remember a guy-- but the name, I don't remember the name.
A guy came last year [BACKROUND NOISE] to the factory from leather, he should bring wood.
He didn't bring the wood.
He says, I cannot bring the wood by my horse-- cannot walk.
And he bring the-- and I sign it.
I have 20, 20 wagons.
And he's the 20th.
I'll sign it.
He says, I'll bring you a chicken.
I'll bring you butter.
I'll bring you everything there.
Next day, he bring me butter.
He bring me chicken.
But the next course, my name is Josef.
And I don't remember the name.
Mm-hmm.
